Item 1. Business
The Boeing Company, together with its subsidiaries (herein referred to as "Boeing," the "Company," "we," "us," "our"), is one of the world's major aerospace firms.
We are organized based on the products and services we offer. We operate in four reportable segments:
- 
Commercial Airplanes (BCA);
- 
Defense, Space & Security (BDS);
- 
Global Services (BGS);
- 
Boeing Capital (BCC).
Commercial Airplanes Segment
This segment develops, produces and markets commercial jet aircraft and provides fleet support services, principally to the commercial airline industry worldwide. We are a leading producer of commercial aircraft and offer a family of commercial jetliners designed to meet a broad spectrum of global passenger and cargo requirements of airlines. This family of commercial jet aircraft in production includes the 737 narrow-body model and the 747, 767, 777 and 787 wide-body models. Development continues on the 777X program and certain 737 MAX derivatives.
Defense, Space & Security Segment
This 
…[truncated 11267 chars]…
merous U.S. government agencies and entities, including but not limited to all of the branches of the U.S. military, NASA, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the Department of Homeland Security. Similar government authorities exist in our non-U.S. markets.
Government Contracts.
The U.S. government, and other governments, may terminate any of our government contracts at their convenience, as well as for default based on our failure to meet specified performance requirements. If any of our U.S. government contracts were to be terminated for convenience, we generally would be entitled to receive payment for work completed and allowable termination or cancellation costs. If any of our government contracts were to be terminated for default, generally the U.S. government would pay only for the work that has been accepted and could require us to pay the difference between the original contract price and the cost to re-procure the contract items, net of the work accepted from the original contract. The U.S. government can also hold us liable for damages resulting from the default.
Commercial Aircraft.
In the U.S., our commercial aircraft products are required to comply with FAA regulations governing production and quality systems, airworthiness and installation approvals, repair procedures and continuing operational safety. Outside the U.S., similar requirements exist for airworthiness, installation and operational approvals. These requirements are generally administered by the national aviation authorities of each country and, in the case of Europe, coordinated by the European Union Aviation Safety Agency.
Environmental.
We are subject to various federal, state, local and non-U.S. laws and regulations relating to environmental protection, including the discharge, treatment, storage, disposal and
4
Table of Contents
remediation of hazardous substances and wastes. We continually assess our compliance status and management of environmental matters to ensure our operations are in compliance with all applicable environmental laws and regulations. Investigation, remediation, and operation and maintenance costs associated with environmental compliance and management of sites are a normal, recurring part of our operations. These costs often are allowable costs under our contracts with the U.S. government. It is reasonably possible that costs incurred to ensure continued environmental compliance could have a material impact on our results of operations, financial condition or cash flows if additional work requirements or more stringent clean-up standards are imposed by regulators, new areas of soil, air and groundwater contamination are discovered and/or expansions of work scope are prompted by the results of investigations.
A Potentially Responsible Party (PRP) has joint and several liability under existing U.S. environmental laws. Where we have been designated a PRP by the Environmental Protection Agency or a state environmental agency, we are potentially liable to the government or third parties for the full cost of remediating contamination at our facilities or former facilities or at third-party sites. If we were required to fully fund the remediation of a site for which we were originally assigned a partial share, the statutory framework would allow us to pursue rights to contribution from other PRPs. For additional information relating to environmental contingencies, see Note 13 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.
Non-U.S. Sales.
Our non-U.S. sales are subject to both U.S. and non-U.S. governmental regulations and procurement policies and practices, including regulations relating to import-export control, tariffs, investment, exchange controls, anti-corruption, and repatriation of earnings. Non-U.S. sales are also subject to varying currency, political and economic risks.
Raw Materials, Parts, and Subassemblies
We are highly dependent on the availability of essential materials, parts and subassemblies from our suppliers and subcontractors. The most important raw materials required for our aerospace products are aluminum (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions), titanium (sheet, plate, forgings and extrusions) and composites (including carbon and boron). Although alternative sources generally exist for these raw materials, qualification of the sources could take a year or more. Many major components and product equipment items are procured or subcontracted on a sole-source basis with a number of companies.
Suppliers
We are dependent upon the ability of a large number of U.S. and non-U.S. suppliers and subcontractors to meet performance specifications, quality standards and delivery schedules at our anticipated costs. While we maintain an extensive qualification and performance surveillance system to control risk associated with such reliance on third parties, failure of suppliers or subcontractors to meet commitments could adversely affect production schedules and program/contract profitability, thereby jeopardizing our ability to fulfill commitments to our customers. We are also dependent on the availability of energy sources, such as electricity, at affordable prices.
Seasonality
No material portion of our business is considered to be seasonal.
Executive Officers of the Registrant
See "Item 10. Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance" in Part III.
5

Other Information
Boeing was originally incorporated in the State of Washington in 1916 and reincorporated in Delaware in 1934. Our principal executive offices are located at 100 N. Riverside Plaza, Chicago, Illinois 60606 and our telephone number is (312) 544-2000.
General information about us can be found at www.boeing.com. The information contained on or connected to our website is not incorporated by reference into this Annual Report on Form 10-K and should not be considered part of this or any other report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). BA.quantitative_report:
### Quantitative Report for BA (as of 2023-01-01)

**1. Revenue, Earnings, Margin, Cash-Flow, and Balance-Sheet Trajectory**  
- **Revenue**: TTM revenue is 61.42B. Latest quarter (2022-09-30) revenue is 15.96B, down QoQ by -4.3% from 16.68B but up YoY by +4.4% from 15.28B. Quarterly revenue has ranged from 13.99B to 17.00B over the last eight quarters.  
- **Earnings**: TTM net income is -8.55B. Latest quarter net income is -3.31B with EPS diluted of -5.49. Earnings are volatile, with positive EPS in Q2 2022 (0.32) and Q2 2021 (1), but mostly negative.  
- **Margins**: Gross margin is -0.014, operating margin -0.12, net margin -0.139. Net margin has been negative throughout 2022, ranging from -0.069 to -0.139 per the sparse history. Gross profit in Q3 2022 is -822.00M.  
- **Cash-Flow**: Free cash flow and cash flow from operations are unavailable for all quarters.  
- **Balance-Sheet Trajectory**: Total equity is -17.64B, total debt is 57.02B, cash is 14.30B. Over eight quarters, total debt decreased from 63.38B (2020-12-31) to 57.02B, but cash decreased from 25.60B to 14.30B. Total assets declined from 152.14B to 137.56B.

**2. Earnings Quality and Balance-Sheet Resilience**  
- **Earnings Quality**: Earnings are low quality, with persistent negative net income, frequent negative gross profit, and volatile EPS. Cash flow data is unavailable to assess cash conversion or accruals.  
- **Balance-Sheet Resilience**: Negative equity of -17.64B indicates insolvency. Net debt (debt minus cash) is approximately 42.72B. High leverage and declining cash pose liquidity risks.

**3. Valuation versus the Company's Own Supplied History**  
- **Valuation Ratios**: Price-to-sales (P/S) is 1.848. Price-to-earnings (P/E), enterprise value to EBIT (EV/EBIT), free cash flow yield, and price-to-book (P/B) are not applicable due to negative earnings and equity.  
- **Historical Context**: Net margin has been negative throughout 2022, ranging from -0.069 to -0.139, making traditional valuation metrics uninformative. No historical P/S data is supplied for comparison.

**4. Price Level and Quarterly/Annual Return Context versus Fundamental Path**  
- **Price Level**: Latest close is 190.49 (2022-12-30). Annual return from 2021-12-30 to 2022-12-30 is -6.0% (from 202.71 to 190.49). Quarterly return from 2022-09-30 to 2022-12-30 is +57.3% (from 121.08 to 190.49).  
- **Fundamental Path**: The quarterly price surge of +57.3% contrasts with Q3 2022 fundamentals showing worsened earnings (net income -3.31B, EPS -5.49). The annual return of -6.0% aligns with poor fundamental trajectory over the year.

**5. Strongest Quantitative Upside and Downside**  
- **Upside**: The only quantitative upside is the recent quarterly price increase of +57.3%, but this is not supported by fundamentals. The P/S ratio of 1.848 is low, but with negative margins, it does not indicate cheapness based on supplied history.  
- **Downside**: Negative equity (-17.64B), persistent losses (TTM net income -8.55B), negative margins, high debt (57.02B), and declining cash (from 25.60B to 14.30B) suggest continued financial distress. Absent cash flow data raises liquidity concerns.

**6. Provisional Quantitative Risk/Reward Conclusion**  
- Based on point-in-time evidence, quantitative risk is high: negative earnings, negative equity, and poor margins indicate fundamental weakness. Reward is not evident from valuation metrics, as P/E and P/B are not applicable, and P/S of 1.848 is not validated by positive profitability. The recent price disconnect from fundamentals suggests volatility. A conclusion on risk/reward is conditional on unobserved cash flow data, but current numbers imply unfavorable risk-reward.
BA.qualitative_report:
**Qualitative Assessment: Boeing (BA)**
**Report Date:** 2023-01-01
**Evidence Cutoff:** 2022-12-31

**1. Business / Franchise Read (from 2021 10-K)**
Boeing is a major global aerospace manufacturer with a diversified business model across four segments:
*   **Commercial Airplanes (BCA):** A leading producer of commercial jetliners (737, 777, 787 families, 777X in development). Faces "extremely competitive" markets from Airbus and emerging competitors (Russia, China, Japan).
*   **Defense, Space & Security (BDS):** Engages in military aircraft, weapons systems, space systems, and satellites. Primary customer is the U.S. Department of Defense (83% of BDS 2020 revenue). Competes directly with Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and SpaceX.
*   **Global Services (BGS):** Provides a full spectrum of lifecycle services for both commercial and defense platforms.
*   **Boeing Capital (BCC):** Manages customer financing and leasing.
The business is heavily regulated (FAA, DoD) and reliant on a complex, global supplier network.

**2. Recent Developments (Dated, from Supplied Text)**
*   **Positive Market Outlook & Analyst Support (2022-12-30):** Morgan Stanley analyst remains bullish on aerospace recovery, noting global air traffic was ~75% of pre-COVID levels (Oct 2022) with full normalization expected in 2023 as China re-opens. Corporate travel budgets were projected to reach 98% of 2019 levels. Analysts' average recommendation was seen as optimistic for the stock.
*   **Defense Contract Win (2022-12-30):** Boeing won a $497 million deal for support of the CH-47 Chinook helicopter.
*   **Turnaround Narrative & Capital Allocation (2022-12-20):** Boeing CEO Dave Calhoun commented on "making important strides" in its turnaround, generating strong cash, and being on a "solid path to achieving positive free cash flow."
*   **Competitive Position Shift (2022-12-31):** A web source noted that following the 737 MAX incidents, Airbus replaced Boeing as the industry leader for sales by revenue.
*   **Human Capital & Legal Challenges (2022-11-17 & 2022-11-22):** Lawsuits accuse Boeing of failing to protect factory workers from COVID-19. Separate lawsuits were filed by other companies attempting to stop Boeing from aggressively recruiting their engineers, indicating a competitive labor market.

**3. Material Qualitative Risks & Offsets**
*   **Risks:**
    *   **Legal & Regulatory:** Ongoing lawsuits related to COVID-19 worker safety (2022-11-17) and past 737 MAX crises, which involved investigations by the FAA, DOJ, and Congress (2022-11-23). The 10-K states contracts can be terminated for default, with significant financial liability.
    *   **Competitive Pressure:** Airbus has overtaken Boeing in sales, and competition in all segments (commercial, defense, services) is characterized as intense and ongoing (10-K, 2022-12-31).
    *   **Supply Chain & Execution:** Heavy dependence on suppliers for materials (titanium, composites) and sole-source components creates production risk (10-K).
    *   **Labor Relations:** Workforce includes significant union membership (~47,000 members in 2020) with major contract expirations in 2022 and 2024 (10-K). Aggressive recruitment may strain industry relationships (2022-11-22).
*   **Offsets:**
    *   **Secular Recovery Tailwind:** Macro view of a commercial aerospace recovery to pre-COVID traffic levels in 2023 (2022-12-30).
    *   **Diversified Revenue Base:** Strong and stable government defense business provides a counter-cyclical buffer (10-K).
    *   **Turnaround Execution:** Management points to improving cash flow and operational strides (2022-12-20).
    *   **Product Portfolio:** Maintains key product lines across commercial and defense (e.g., CH-47 contract win).

**4. Qualitative Assessment**
**Cautiously Positive.**
The near-term narrative is supported by a cyclical recovery in air travel and a management focus on financial turnaround, which appears to be generating positive analyst sentiment. The company's diversified defense business provides a partial hedge. However, the franchise faces material, ongoing risks from intense competition, complex supply chains, significant legal and regulatory headwinds, and the need to execute flawlessly on its recovery plan amidst a challenging labor environment. The positive assessment is contingent on the continued normalization of global air travel and the successful mitigation of its persistent operational and legal challenges.

corrections.issue: Qualitative report states 'Morgan Stanley analyst remains bullish on aerospace recovery' and frames this as a positive offset, but the raw evidence shows this is a general sector call about aerospace recovery, not a specific BA buy recommendation. The raw news also notes 'Wall Street analysts' recommendations tend to be overly optimistic' (2022-12-28 article), which the qualitative report omits.
corrections.required_action: PM should note that analyst bullishness is sector-level, not BA-specific, and the raw evidence itself flags analyst optimism bias.

corrections.issue: Qualitative report cites CEO Calhoun's statement about 'generating strong cash' and being on a 'solid path to achieving positive free cash flow' as a turnaround offset. However, the quantitative pack shows cash flow data is entirely unavailable (all quarters show 'no data'), and cash on the balance sheet declined from 25.60B to 14.30B over eight quarters. The CEO claim cannot be verified or contradicted from supplied evidence, but the balance sheet trend does not corroborate 'strong cash generation.'
corrections.required_action: PM should treat management's cash flow claim as unverifiable from supplied data and note the declining cash balance as a countervailing signal.

corrections.issue: Quantitative report states 'Net margin has been negative throughout 2022, ranging from -0.069 to -0.139' which is consistent with the sparse history. However, the report also says 'Gross margin is -0.014' as a point-in-time figure without noting that gross profit was positive in 4 of the 8 quarters shown (Q2 2022: 2.12B, Q1 2022: 346M, Q2 2021: 2.41B, Q1 2021: 1.41B). The TTM gross profit of -868M masks significant quarterly volatility. The quant report's characterization of 'frequent negative gross profit' is slightly overstated—it is 4 of 8 quarters negative.
corrections.required_action: PM should note gross margin is volatile (positive in 4/8 quarters) rather than uniformly negative, which matters for any recovery scenario.

unsupported_claims: : Qualitative report states 'management focus on financial turnaround, which appears to be generating positive analyst sentiment' — the raw evidence shows analyst sentiment is described as optimistic but is sector-level (aerospace recovery) and the raw evidence itself questions whether analyst recommendations are effective (2022-12-28 article). The causal link 'turnaround generating positive sentiment' is not established in the supplied text.

: Qualitative report characterizes the assessment as 'Cautiously Positive' based partly on 'secular recovery tailwind' and 'turnaround execution.' The quantitative report concludes 'unfavorable risk-reward.' These are not strictly contradictory (one is qualitative framing, the other quantitative), but the qualitative report's positive lean is not well-grounded in the supplied financials, which show negative equity, persistent losses, and no verifiable cash flow improvement.

pm_decision_obj.executive_summary: Boeing's structural duopoly and massive order backlog provide long-term franchise value, but severe balance sheet distress (negative equity of -$17.64B, net debt of ~$42.72B) and persistent losses (TTM net income of -$8.55B) create substantial near-term risk. The stock's recent +57.3% quarterly surge appears disconnected from fundamentals, leaving the risk-reward roughly balanced at current levels.
pm_decision_obj.investment_thesis: Boeing's structural duopoly and massive order backlog provide long-term franchise value, but severe balance sheet distress (negative equity of -$17.64B, net debt of ~$42.72B) and persistent losses (TTM net income of -$8.55B) create substantial near-term risk. The stock's recent +57.3% quarterly surge appears disconnected from fundamentals, leaving the risk-reward roughly balanced at current levels.
key_factors: Structural franchise value: Boeing holds a global duopoly in widebody commercial aircraft with a 3,365-unit 737 order book and 405-unit 787 backlog, plus a diversified defense business (83% of BDS revenue from U.S. DoD) providing counter-cyclical stability.

Severe balance sheet distress: Total equity is -$17.64B (2022-09-30) with total debt of $57.02B against cash of $14.30B, yielding net debt of ~$42.72B. Cash has declined from $25.60B to $14.30B over eight quarters, raising liquidity concerns despite management's claim of 'strong cash generation.'

Persistent negative profitability: TTM net income is -$8.55B with net margin of -0.139 in Q3 2022. Gross margin is volatile (positive in 4 of 8 quarters) but the TTM gross profit is -$868M, indicating the turnaround remains incomplete.

Price-fundamental disconnect: The stock surged +57.3% in Q4 2022 (from $121.08 to $190.49) while Q3 fundamentals worsened (net income -$3.31B, EPS -$5.49). The P/S of 1.848 is low but uninformative given negative earnings and equity, leaving valuation ambiguous.
